title: Engineering Quantum Criticality in the Integer Quantum Hall Regime through a Screening Layer
authors: C. T. Tai, P. T. Madathil, A. Gupta, L. N. Pfeiffer, K. W. Baldwin, M. Shayegan
abstract: Disorder-induced localization of electrons and electron-electron interaction are among the most fundamental problems in condensed matter physics. In two-dimensional electron systems, extensive studies have led to the emergence of a scaling picture, characterized by a set of universal critical exponents that govern the transitions between the integer quantum Hall plateaus. From the temperature dependence of the plateau-to-plateau transitions, experiments primarily report k ~ 0.42, implying a dynamic exponent z = 1, consistent with a theoretical picture where electrons have a long-range (1/r) interaction. Theory also predicts that z = 2 for short-range electron interaction, but an experimental verification has remained elusive. Here, we directly probe the influence of Coulomb interaction on these transitions using a bilayer electron system confined to a GaAs double quantum well device. The two layers are in close proximity, with an interlayer distance approximately equal to the magnetic length at the relevant magnetic fields. By tuning the electron density in the top layer, we access both insulating and metallic phases of the electrons in this layer as a function of magnetic field, allowing in-situ control of the unscreened and screened interaction strengths in the bottom layer as it goes through its plateau-to-plateau transitions. In the unscreened case, we measure k ~ 0.42 consistent with the widely reported value. More importantly, when screening is introduced, k is reduced to ~ 0.22, implying z = 2. Our results provide direct experimental evidence for the role of electron-electron interaction in determining critical behavior in the quantum Hall regime, and demonstrate screening as a powerful tuning parameter for engineering quantum criticality.
summary: This work experimentally probes quantum criticality in the Integer Quantum Hall Effect by using a bilayer electron system where the interaction strength can be controlled via an overlying screening layer. By measuring the critical exponent $\kappa$, the authors demonstrate a clear transition from behavior indicative of long-range interactions to that of short-range interactions upon screening, providing crucial evidence for interaction effects in this regime.
main_problem: The study investigates how electron-electron interaction, specifically through screening, dictates the critical behavior (quantum criticality) during plateau-to-plateau transitions in the Integer Quantum Hall Effect (IQHE) regime.
main_result: The measurements provide direct evidence that screening the electron-electron interaction reduces the critical exponent $\kappa$ from a value consistent with long-range interactions ($z=1$) to a value consistent with short-range interactions ($z=2$).
method: The critical exponent $\kappa$ is extracted by analyzing the temperature dependence of the derivative of the Hall resistance ($	ext{d}R_{xy}/	ext{d}B$) at the transition points.
model_or_system: The experiment uses a GaAs double quantum well device containing two closely spaced 2D electron systems (top and bottom layers). The top layer density is tuned to control the screening environment for the bottom layer.
key_observables: The critical exponent $\kappa$, the Hall resistance $R_{xy}$ vs. magnetic field $B$, and the temperature dependence of these measurements.
important_parameters: The degree of screening, controlled by the top layer density ($n_t$), and the magnetic field strength relative to the magnetic length ($l_B$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on established scaling relations connecting the critical exponent $\kappa$ to the dynamic exponent $z$ based on the nature of the electron interaction (long-range vs. short-range).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the sample structure, the temperature dependence of $	ext{d}R_{xy}/	ext{d}B$ for varying screening conditions, and the resulting extracted values of $\kappa$ for both unscreened and screened regimes.
paper_structure: The paper systematically measures the critical exponent $\kappa$ by varying the screening environment (top layer density) and comparing the results to theoretical predictions for different interaction ranges.
why_interesting: The ability to tune the interaction strength via an external gate voltage provides a clean experimental handle to test fundamental theoretical predictions regarding the universality class governing quantum critical points in strongly correlated electron systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic of the sample structure, consisting of two 18-nm-wide GaAs QWs separated by a 5-nm-wide AlAs barrier. (b) Charge distribution (red) and potential energy (black), calculated by solving the Schr¨odinger-Poisson equa- tions self-consistently. (c) Schematic of the device structure. The gray area represents the Hall bar mesa, and the green hexagonal areas are the ohmic contacts which are used as voltage probes, or for injecting current through source (S) and drain (D). Finger gates 1-4 are used to deplete the top layer so that magnetotransport coefficients of the bottom layer can be measured. The global top gate (Vt) allows changing the top-layer electron density.

FIG. 2. Summary of the measured κ values in this work. (a) Temperature dependence of dRxy/dB|B=Bc for different transitions, labeled A-H . Blue symbols are used for cases A- E where there is no screening provided by the top layer, and red symbols for cases F-H where there is screening. The data for each case are shifted vertically for clarity. Panels (b)-(e) show transitions C-H schematically. Black (green) curves represent the top-layer (bottom-layer) Rxy traces, and νt and νb indicate their respective filling factors. Panel (f) presents the Rxy traces for the bottom layer near integer quantum Hall plateaus νb = 2 to 4 as a function of temperature ranging from 0.30 K to 1.12 K when the top...

FIG. 3. Temperature dependence of Rxy for the top layer (dotted traces) and bottom layer (solid traces). Panels (a)-(c) show the bottom layer plateau-to-plateau transitions at nt = 5.0, 4.1, and 2.5, respectively, with the bottom-layer density fixed at nb = 6.5.

FIG. 4. Measured κ for the plateau-to-plateau transitions νb = 2 to 3 and 3 to 4 as a function of the top-layer density nt. In panel (a), κ are shown for cases A–H , with the corre- sponding nt values indicated by green vertical lines, together with additional data points in their vicinity, illustrating the evolution of κ with nt. Blue and red symbols represent the unscreened and screened, respectively. Square symbols corre- spond to the νb = 2 to 3 transition, while circles represent the νb = 3 to 4 transition.
